title: VPN Kill Switch: What It Is and Why You Need It
description: A VPN kill switch blocks your internet connection if the VPN drops, preventing accidental exposure. Here is how it works and which VPNs have the best implementation.
content:
The Problem a Kill Switch Solves

VPN connections can drop without warning. A sudden disconnection can happen because of a server issue, your device switching networks, or a temporary internet outage. When a VPN drops, your traffic reverts to your regular unprotected connection. This can expose your real IP address and your traffic to anyone monitoring the network, including your ISP.

What a Kill Switch Does

A kill switch detects when the VPN connection drops and immediately blocks all internet traffic until the VPN reconnects. Your device appears to go offline rather than continue browsing unprotected. Some implementations block only the apps you specify. Others block all traffic system-wide. System-wide is the more secure option.

When You Need a Kill Switch

If you use a VPN only for streaming, a kill switch matters less. If your connection drops during a Netflix session, you notice and reconnect. But if you use a VPN for privacy, torrenting, or working on sensitive projects, a kill switch prevents accidental exposure during those moments when you are not actively watching. It is an important feature for anyone who relies on their VPN for consistent privacy rather than just occasional use.

VPNs with Strong Kill Switch Implementations

NordVPN has a reliable system-wide kill switch on all platforms including mobile. ExpressVPN calls its implementation Network Lock and it works well on desktop. Mullvad has one of the most consistent implementations and is trusted by privacy-focused users. ProtonVPN includes a kill switch even on its free tier. Most major VPNs now include kill switches, but the quality and reliability vary. Check that the feature is enabled by default or enable it in settings.

Testing Your Kill Switch

To test if your kill switch is working, connect to a VPN, then disconnect the VPN manually while keeping your browser open. If the kill switch is active, all web traffic should immediately stop. If pages continue to load, the kill switch either is not enabled or is not working properly. Some VPNs let you simulate a connection drop in the app settings for exactly this kind of test.
